one_line_takeaway: A practical, multi-cloud, containerized DFIR system that bundles Random Forest network/malware classification, an Isolation-Forest web-IDS, and an escalation LSTM into a deployed pipeline, with strong RF accuracy on CIC-IDS-2017 (99.82%) but engineering-integration novelty rather than algorithmic advance, and per-component (not end-to-end) evaluation.
key_finding: Random Forest was the strongest classical model across tasks: 90.92% (NSL-KDD), 75.64% (UNSW-NB15), and 99.82% (CIC-IDS-2017) accuracy for network traffic classification, and 96.71% accuracy for malware detection. The secondary LSTM/Keras model reached ~99% accuracy on malware but was demoted to a reserve/escalation role because of its high inference-time compute cost. The WIDS with contamination=0.01 balanced sensitivity against false positives and successfully flagged a live Nikto directory-traversal attack on Azure, triggering logging and email alerts. Attack-simulation results were mixed by vector: brute force, fuzzing, port scan and SQL injection were detected well, while Metasploit exploits (UNSW-NB15 model) and an R2L FTP-write/php-webshell case (NSL-KDD model) showed low detection and/or high false positives.
core_contribution: The paper proposes and deploys 'Periscope-AI', a unified, containerized AI/ML-driven cyber incident detection and response (DFIR) system for public cloud environments, integrating three independent applications: (1) a real-time network traffic classifier (Random Forest over PCAP-derived features), (2) a web intrusion detection system (WIDS) using Isolation Forest anomaly detection over HTTP server logs, and (3) a dual-model malware analysis Flask app (Random Forest primary + a Keras/TensorFlow LSTM secondary) for static analysis of uploaded .exe files. The claimed novelty is not a new ML algorithm but the end-to-end engineering: a three-tier VPC architecture (Production, Honeypot with T-Pot, DFIR) deployed on Google Cloud and Azure using Docker/Kubernetes, with an ELK stack for log visualization and a honeypot-fed continuous retraining pipeline. The authors position this against prior cloud-forensics/AI-DFIR work (SCARF, FROST, DFaaS, and various surveys reviewed in Related Work) as being the one that combines multi-cloud deployment, a honeypot data source, and real practical implementation rather than a theoretical framework.
threat_model: Defensive detection-and-response system for enterprise cloud infrastructure. Adversary is a generic external/internal attacker launching network- and web-layer attacks (DoS/DDoS, brute force, port scan, SQL injection, fuzzing, exploits, R2L, path/directory traversal) and delivering malicious executables. The system aims to detect these in real time via mirrored network traffic, forwarded HTTP logs, and file uploads/storage-bucket scans, then alert analysts. The paper does not consider adversarial ML evasion, poisoning of the honeypot-fed retraining pipeline, or attacks against the detection system itself; its evaluation adversary is a red-teamer using Kali Linux pentest tools against deliberately vulnerable hosts.
limitations: Author-stated: system has not been evaluated under comprehensive/diverse real-world threat scenarios (only known attack types on deliberately vulnerable hosts); anomaly-detection notion of 'normal' behavior is hard to define and degrades at large data volumes; false positives remain a concern; honeypot-based continuous retraining is only a proposed concept, not evaluated. Reviewer-added: the three components are evaluated in isolation with no unified end-to-end metric or latency/throughput numbers despite real-time claims; the WIDS is validated on a private/unnamed HTTP-log dataset with only anecdotal detection evidence (no precision/recall reported); the malware dataset is small (~991 benign, ~428 malware from VirusTotal, Windows-biased) and the reported 96% vs 99% figures are rounded versions of Table VI's 96.71% (RF) and the ~99% LSTM claim; UNSW-NB15 accuracy of 75.64% is weak; no comparison of the classifiers' numbers against external state-of-the-art on the same benchmarks (the 'comparison with related works' is a qualitative spider/radar chart of novelty/completeness/innovativeness, not accuracy).

datasets_used: NSL-KDD (network traffic; 125,973 training / 22,544 testing samples, 41 features, 38 attack categories)
UNSW-NB15 (network traffic; 175,341 training / 82,332 testing samples, 49 features)
CIC-IDS-2017 (network traffic; ~2.8M samples, 80 features, 80/20 split ~2.24M train / ~560k test)
VirusTotal-sourced malware dataset (Benignware: 694 train + 297 test; Malware: 299 train + 129 test; 70/30 split)
Private/unnamed HTTP server access-log dataset used for the WIDS Isolation Forest
quantitative_results: Network traffic Random Forest accuracy: NSL-KDD 90.92%, UNSW-NB15 75.64%, CIC-IDS-2017 99.82%
Network traffic Decision Tree accuracy: NSL-KDD 87.13%, UNSW-NB15 73.95%, CIC-IDS 99.76%
Network traffic Logistic Regression accuracy: NSL-KDD 85.75%, UNSW-NB15 63.16%, CIC-IDS 96.2%
Network traffic KNN accuracy: NSL-KDD 85.34%, UNSW-NB15 60.33%, CIC-IDS 99.22%
Network traffic Naive Bayes accuracy: NSL-KDD 51.58%, UNSW-NB15 41.98%, CIC-IDS 69.60%
Malware Random Forest: 96.71% accuracy, 94.44% precision, 94.44% recall, 94.44% F1
Malware SVM: 91.54% accuracy, 95.91% precision, 74.60% recall, 83.92% F1
Malware Logistic Regression: 94.60% accuracy, 97.24% precision, 84.12% recall, 90.21% F1
Malware Decision Tree: 88.26% accuracy, 87.25% precision, 70.63% recall, 78.07% F1
Secondary Keras/LSTM malware model: ~99% accuracy (used only as escalation model due to high inference compute)
Malware classification thresholds: >0.7 probability = Malicious; 0.5-0.7 escalate to LSTM; below = Benign
WIDS Isolation Forest: contamination 0.01 selected as best of {0.001, 0.01, 0.1}; sample size raised 1,000 -> 10,000; random_state=100
baselines_compared: Network traffic classification: Random Forest vs Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, KNN, Naive Bayes (own Table II)
Malware analysis: Random Forest vs Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree (own Table VI); RF vs Keras/LSTM neural network

Qualitative-only comparison (novelty/completeness/innovativeness spider chart) against prior cloud-DFIR works reviewed in Related Work (SCARF, FROST, DFaaS/FaaS, Zewdie CNN+SVM, etc.) — no head-to-head accuracy comparison against external state of the art
